transcript.clean_text: La Vuelta reaching a critical point with the Tourmalet beckoning on stage 13. Before then, well, perhaps something of a an easier day for the GC men. Destination Zaragoza, surely one for the sprinters. Wide open plains, no mountain points up for grabs. Only the wind could possibly disturb matters, but that ebbed as we flowed into today's 12th stage. Temperatures just about perfect. Nothing too hot, you understand, until the very end. 150.6 km in total. Zaragoza with a technical finale on wide open highways beckoning. Plus an intermediate sprint with bonus seconds up for grabs and plenty of points as well. At the beginning of the day, well, it was not going to be much of a temptation for a mass breakaway because it was always going to be controlled by the sprinters' teams. And as it was, Balderstone from Caja Rural, the 23-year-old, and yet to Ball, 10 years his senior, went up the road. Ball has spent many years in Spain riding for Burgos BH. Today, well, there were some rough moments, I have to tell you. Occasionally, little bit of brain fade, wheels touch, and down we went. That was about all the drama we had. Thankfully, nobody badly hurt, and we raced on. The wide open plains, even the wind farms here were not turning too brightly. 44 km from home, our breakaway was caught, and then Ball decided he hadn't quite had enough. There was a combativity jersey up for grabs. He's been in the breaker few times already during this race. Maybe he's thinking overall combativity and why not. And so it was with just under 40 km to go, the opposite of Kärnä had done quite a bit of work on behalf of Kaden Groves. Would they have burnt too many matches? Well, if there was intermediate sprint points up for grabs, he would take those and pretty much Roglič was determined to take some bonus seconds. If there's a penny on the floor, why not pick it up? Well, Roglič did four seconds to the good. And despite the teammate of Remco Evenepoel trying to limit the damage of that intermediate sprint, Roglič moving closer to the Belgian in the overall. So it was we came into town, onto the ring road, and some looping curves, very much high-paced, and Kaden Groves the out-and-out favorite. We crossed the river, took an enormous U-turn, and then slowly but surely the road bent towards the left and our finale. The hot wheel was of course Kaden Groves. Everyone wanted to be on it. Once we'd passed the 3 km to go marker, all the GC players, they indeed took their leave, just happy to roll in. And now the battle was about to commence. Lots of shoulders out there, wheels being fought for. Alaphilippe and Soudal still full of belief after going so well on the run on stage seven. Meanwhile, the big pickup was UAE Team Emirates. Rui Oliveira absolutely screaming up into space. Milan finding plenty, and he had the drive to deliver. Coming out into the middle of the road, Kaden Groves but a dropped a chain at a crucial moment. Coming home in third place, Boy van Poppel was supposed to be working for Hugo Houle, who got trapped like so many on the twists and turns of the approach. Huge day for Molano, then, and indeed for Colombia. Guided in beautifully by Rui Oliveira. Spectacularly so. Well, they said that the shortest route home, if it gently curves to the left, is, of course, on the left, but they went into the middle of the road, coming up the right-hand side of the pack, and it was space that was the frontier that they really wanted to master, and they did. Great win for UAE Team Emirates, and of course, they still have Ayuso, Almeida, and Marc Soler very much involved in the general classification. Molano taking the head against Groves, Van Poppel, Oliveira, Tunes, Vandenbergh, Dainese, Alaphilippe, Page, and Menten. Leader remains McNulty. 26 seconds of advantage over Marc Soler. Even the pool is at a minute and nine, and a reduced margin. 1 minute and 32 down is Primoz Roglic. Kaden Groves, well, he's extended his lead in the green jersey competition. Let's hope he doesn't start ruing some of those gaps, but he's over 200 points now. 208. Indeed, 200 clear of his nearest rival. Looks good. Col du Tourmalet beckons, and not only that, we have the Aubisque, the Spandelles, and more. We're climbing straight out of the back door tomorrow. And this day has virtually no flat ground within it. A massive day for the general classification. Hey.
